editable_fields.label: Tip 1 — detail
editable_fields.default: Every other vendor — caterer, decorator, photographer — quotes you based on the venue. Lock it 9-12 months out and the rest of the planning gets 40% easier.

editable_fields.label: Tip 2 — detail
editable_fields.default: Every desi wedding has the same 13 phases — engagement, save-the-date, vendors, outfits, mehendi, sangeet, ceremony, reception, after. Map them to dates early so nothing slips.

editable_fields.label: Tip 3 — detail
editable_fields.default: WhatsApp voice notes are not a brief. Send every vendor a one-page brief — names, timings, family preferences, must-haves, hard nos. The day-of runs on this document.

editable_fields.label: Tip 4 — detail
editable_fields.default: Mehendi will run long. Baraat will start late. The pandit will arrive when the muhurat says, not when you say. Add a 30% buffer to every transition and you'll stay on time.

editable_fields.label: Tip 5 — detail
editable_fields.default: Even if you plan everything yourself, hire someone for the day-of. You should not be the one taking calls about missing chairs while you're in your bridal lehenga.

editable_fields.label: Tip 1 — detail
editable_fields.default: May–September is monsoon-shoulder pricing in most cities. Same venue, 25-40% off. Same vendors, more attention.

editable_fields.label: Tip 2 — detail
editable_fields.default: Every 50 guests is roughly ₹2-3 lakh in catering, decor, and welcome kits. The list, not the lehenga, is the budget lever.

editable_fields.label: Tip 3 — detail
editable_fields.default: Lock the same decorator for sangeet + ceremony + reception. Bundled bookings save 10-15% vs. event-by-event quotes.

editable_fields.label: Tip 4 — title
editable_fields.default: Rent The Mandap, Not The Trends

editable_fields.label: Tip 4 — detail
editable_fields.default: Custom-built mandaps cost 3x rentals and you'll never use them again. Rent the structure, splurge on the florals.

editable_fields.label: Tip 5 — detail
editable_fields.default: Digital STDs land in the WhatsApp group in 2 minutes. Save the print budget for the actual invite suite.

editable_fields.label: Tip 6 — detail
editable_fields.default: Premium liquor for the first hour, switch to house pours after. Nobody is judging the second drink — they're dancing.

editable_fields.label: Tip 7 — title
editable_fields.default: Rent The Outfits You'll Wear Once

editable_fields.label: Tip 7 — detail
editable_fields.default: The reception gown? Rent. The mehendi outfit? Rent. The wedding lehenga is the only one most brides actually re-wear.

editable_fields.label: Tip 8 — detail
editable_fields.default: Mid-week dates can drop venue cost by 30%. Talk to your pandit — auspicious slots exist on Tuesdays too.

editable_fields.label: Tip 9 — detail
editable_fields.default: Plan to spend 88% of your stated budget on line items. The other 12% is for the surprises that always come.

editable_fields.label: Tip 10 — detail

editable_fields.default: Mehndi + Sangeet + Wedding + Reception is four events to one vendor. The per-event rate drops 20-30% when bundled. Always ask.

editable_fields.label: Tip 3 — detail
editable_fields.default: Three written quotes for the same scope. Share the lowest with the others — politely. Real vendors will match or explain why their price is higher.

editable_fields.label: Tip 4 — detail
editable_fields.default: Get every deliverable on paper — counts, formats, deadlines. 'Edited photos' isn't a deliverable. '500 edited photos in 6 weeks' is.

editable_fields.label: Ceremonies — content
editable_fields.default: A Punjabi wedding stretches across at least four events: Roka, Sangeet, Mehendi, and the wedding day itself with Anand Karaj or pheras. Expect a baraat with a horse, dhol players, and a procession that takes its time.

editable_fields.label: Fashion — title
editable_fields.default: Lehengas, paranda, and a sherwani for him

editable_fields.label: Fashion — content
editable_fields.default: Brides typically wear a heavily embroidered red or pink lehenga with kundan jewelry, a kaleera tied to the wrist, and a paranda braided into the hair. The groom wears a sherwani with a sehra or safa turban.

editable_fields.label: Food — title
editable_fields.default: Sarson da saag, butter chicken, and the chaat counter

editable_fields.label: Food — content
editable_fields.default: Punjabi weddings live and die by the food. The buffet is non-negotiable: tandoori spreads, paneer in three forms, kulfi falooda, and a live chaat counter that runs all night. Dessert is jalebi with rabri, always.

editable_fields.label: Music — title
editable_fields.default: Dhol, bhangra, and the DJ that knows every Diljit drop

editable_fields.label: Music — content
editable_fields.default: Live dhol players lead the baraat. The sangeet is choreographed by the cousins. Expect bhangra, gidda, and a DJ who can flip from old-school Daler Mehndi to Diljit without losing the dance floor for a single beat.

editable_fields.label: Details — title
editable_fields.default: Kaleere, joota chupai, and the chura ceremony

editable_fields.label: Details — content
editable_fields.default: The chura ceremony — red and white bangles slipped on by the bride's mama — is signature Punjabi. Joota chupai (stealing the groom's shoes) is a friendly ransom war. And kaleere are dropped on cousins to bless the next bride.
